plants.note: Argentine-specific Haplopappus species for bailahuén; distinct from Chilean Haplopappus spp. entry
Aloysia triphylla and Aloysia citrodora are treated as synonyms by many authorities; this entry covers the Argentine binomial used in this source
Argentine book cites E. giganteum; Chilean DS N°3/10 uses E. bogotense for the same common name cola de caballo — see ambiguity flag
Chenopodium ambrosioides is now a synonym of Dysphania ambrosioides; Argentine book retains old binomial. Chilean paico entry uses Chenopodium chilense, a different species

names.name: Abrojo grande
names.ambiguity_note: Abrojo in Argentina may refer to Xanthium cavanillesii (abrojo grande) or Xanthium spinosum (cepa caballo/abrojo chico); size qualifier required

names.name: Abrojo
names.ambiguity_note: Without qualifier, abrojo in Argentina is ambiguous between X. cavanillesii and X. spinosum

names.name: Molle
names.ambiguity_note: Molle in Argentina refers to at least three different species: Schinus molle (aguaribay), Schinus terebinthifolius (molle misionero), and Lithraea molleoides (molle de beber). Country-specific and regional qualifier required

names.name: Algarrobo blanco
names.ambiguity_note: Multiple Prosopis species share algarrobo names in Argentina; algarrobo blanco most consistently = P. alba

names.name: Amaranto
names.ambiguity_note: Amaranto covers several Amaranthus species; A. caudatus is cited in this Argentine source but A. quitensis (yuyo colorado) and A. hybridus share the same space

names.name: Amor seco
names.ambiguity_note: Amor seco in Argentina is Bidens pilosa; the same name in Chile refers to Acaena splendens. Entirely different species

names.name: Anacahuita
names.ambiguity_note: Anacahuita in Argentina = Blepharocalyx salicifolius. In Uruguay it is also applied to this species. In Mexico anacahuita = Cordia boissieri, a completely different plant

names.name: Bailahuén
names.ambiguity_note: Same common name bailahuén as Chile but maps to Haplopappus rigidus in Argentina vs. multiple Haplopappus spp. in Chile; functionally equivalent use but taxonomically distinct

names.name: Contrayerba
names.ambiguity_note: Contrayerba in this Argentine source = Dorstenia brasiliensis. Chilean DS N°3/10 maps contrayerba to Flaveria bidentis. Classical use = Dorstenia contrayerva. Three different species share this name across three countries

names.name: Cadillo
names.ambiguity_note: Cadillo is also a common name for Xanthium cavanillesii (abrojo grande) in Argentina

names.name: Canchalagua
names.ambiguity_note: Canchalagua in Argentina (this source) = Schkuhria pinnata. In Chile canchalagua / cachanlagua = Centaurium cachanlahuen. In Peru canchalagua = Gentianella alborosea or related Gentianaceae. Three countries, three different plants under one name

names.name: Canelo
names.ambiguity_note: Same as Chile; Drimys winteri confirmed in both countries. Global confusion with Cinnamomum (cinnamon) persists

names.name: Mastuerzo
names.ambiguity_note: This Argentine book lists a separate entry 'Mastuerzo' = Coronopus didymus, distinct from the Chilean DS entry which maps mastuerzo to Capsella bursa-pastoris. Additionally, the same Argentine book maps capuchina/mastuerzo to Tropaeolum majus. Three plants share this name

names.name: Cardo amarillo
names.ambiguity_note: Cardo amarillo / cardo santo in Argentina = Argemone mexicana; in other countries cardo amarillo may refer to Centaurea melitensis or Scolymus hispanicus

names.name: Cepa caballo
names.ambiguity_note: In Argentina cepa caballo = Xanthium spinosum. In Chile cepa caballo = Acaena splendens. Completely different species share this name across the border

names.name: Cabello de ángel
names.ambiguity_note: Cabello de ángel in Argentina = Tillandsia usneoides (Spanish moss). In Chile cabello de ángel = Cuscuta chilensis. Two completely different plants share this name

names.name: Cola de caballo
names.ambiguity_note: Argentine source cites Equisetum giganteum; Chilean DS uses E. bogotense; other sources use E. arvense. All share the name cola de caballo

names.name: Chachacoma
names.ambiguity_note: Chachacoma in Argentina = Senecio graveolens; in Bolivia and Chile chachacoma may refer to related Senecio species or even Senna spp.

names.name: Mil hombres
names.ambiguity_note: Milhombres / mil hombres is shared by Aristolochia triangularis and Cissampelos pareira in Argentina

names.name: Chilca
names.ambiguity_note: Chilca is one of the most ambiguous names in South American herbalism, applied to dozens of Baccharis, Eupatorium, and Senecio species across Argentina, Chile, Peru, and Bolivia

names.name: Duraznillo
names.ambiguity_note: Duraznillo is used for multiple toxic plants in Argentina; Colliguaya integerrima (duraznillo de campo) must be distinguished from Cestrum parqui variants also called duraznillo negro

names.name: Espinillo
names.ambiguity_note: Espinillo / aromo in Argentina covers Acacia caven and Acacia aroma; both are used medicinally under these overlapping common names

names.name: Guaco
names.ambiguity_note: Guaco in Argentina is Mikania periplocifolia; in Colombia and Venezuela guaco commonly = Mikania guaco; different species in the same genus

names.name: Quillay
names.ambiguity_note: Quillay in Argentina = Sapindus saponaria (jaboncillo). In Chile quillay = Quillaja saponaria. Two different saponin-rich species from different families share this name across the Andes

names.name: Jarilla
names.ambiguity_note: Jarilla in Argentina covers Larrea divaricata, L. cuneifolia, and L. nitida; the same as noted in Phase 1

names.name: Lapacho
names.ambiguity_note: Lapacho in Argentina most commonly = Tabebuia impetiginosa (lapacho rosado); lapacho amarillo = T. serratifolia. The commercial lapacho bark traded internationally may come from either species

names.name: Vira vira
names.ambiguity_note: Vira vira in Argentina = Achyrocline satureioides (marcela). In Chile vira vira = Pseudognaphalium viravira. Related Asteraceae but distinct genera and species

names.name: Mastuerzo
names.ambiguity_note: See mastuerzo ambiguity note above: Coronopus didymus (this entry), Capsella bursa-pastoris (Chile), and Tropaeolum majus (capuchina/mastuerzo in both Argentina and Chile) all share this name

names.name: Milhombres
names.ambiguity_note: Milhombres in Argentina is primarily Aristolochia triangularis but also applied to Cissampelos pareira (charrúa)

names.name: Muérdago criollo
names.ambiguity_note: Muérdago criollo = Ligaria cuneifolia in Argentina. The name muérdago alone refers to Viscum album (European mistletoe) in standard Spanish; Argentine criollo qualifier disambiguates

names.name: Muña muña
names.ambiguity_note: Muña muña in Argentina = Satureja parvifolia; in Peru and Bolivia muña = Minthostachys mollis or related species (see Round 1 muña entry). Related Lamiaceae but different genera

names.name: Uña de gato
names.ambiguity_note: Uña de gato in Argentina covers at least two completely unrelated plants: Mulinum spinosum (neneo, a thorny cushion shrub from Patagonia) and Uncaria tomentosa (cat's claw vine from Amazonia). This is one of the most dangerous name ambiguities in the database — same name, different continent of origin, vastly different pharmacological profiles

names.name: Paico
names.ambiguity_note: Argentine paico = Chenopodium ambrosioides (now Dysphania ambrosioides). Chilean paico = Chenopodium chilense per DS N°3/10. Both are Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae and share culinary/medicinal uses but are distinct species. The canonical dysphania_ambrosioides entry covers the Argentine use more accurately

names.name: Palo azul
names.ambiguity_note: Palo azul in Argentina = Cyclolepis genistoides; in Mexico palo azul = Eysenhardtia polystachya, a different plant also used as a diuretic

names.name: Palo santo
names.ambiguity_note: Palo santo in Argentina = Bulnesia sarmientoi (dense hardwood from the Chaco). In Chile palo santo = Porlieria chilensis. In Peru and Ecuador palo santo = Bursera graveolens (the aromatic incense tree). Three countries, three completely different species, all called palo santo

names.name: Matico
names.ambiguity_note: In Argentina matico = Buddleja globosa (same as Chile). In Peru matico = Piper aduncum. Confirmed cross-border ambiguity

names.name: Pingo pingo
names.ambiguity_note: The Argentine book lists both Ephedra americana (pingo pingo) and Ephedra triandra (tramontana) as separate entries, but both share some common names regionally. Chilean pingo pingo = Ephedra chilensis, a third species

names.name: Piñón
names.ambiguity_note: Piñón in Argentina = Jatropha curcas (toxic). In other contexts piñón = pine nut (Pinus spp.). Potentially dangerous ambiguity due to toxicity of Jatropha

names.name: Poleo
names.ambiguity_note: Poleo in Argentina = Lippia turbinata (té criollo); in Chile poleo = Mentha pulegium; in other Andean countries poleo may refer to Lippia integrifolia or Satureja spp. This is one of the most regionally unstable herb names in South America

names.name: Jarilla macho
names.ambiguity_note: Jarilla macho = Zuccagnia punctata; jarilla alone = Larrea divaricata or congeners; these are different families

names.name: Sombra de toro
names.ambiguity_note: Sombra de toro in Argentina = Jodina rhombifolia (Santalaceae). The same name in Chile is applied to Maytenus species; also quilembay (Chuquiraga avellanedae) is called sombra de toro in Patagonia

names.name: Torito
names.ambiguity_note: Torito = Acanthospermum hispidum in this source; but yerba del pollo (Alternanthera pungens) is also called torito in Argentina

names.name: Uvilla
names.ambiguity_note: Uvilla in Argentina = Cissus sicyoides (insulina vegetal). In Andean countries uvilla = Physalis peruviana (goldenberry), a completely different plant

names.name: Verbena
names.ambiguity_note: Verbena in Argentina = Verbena littoralis (same species as Chilean Verbena litoralis); Verbena officinalis (European) also present in the region under the same name

names.name: Yerba dulce
names.ambiguity_note: Yerba dulce in Argentina = Stevia rebaudiana (ka'a he'ê in Guaraní). In Chile hierba dulce = Calceolaria thyrsiflora. Completely different plants sharing a translation of 'sweet herb'

names.name: Ataco
names.ambiguity_note: Ataco is used for multiple Amaranthus species across South America including A. quitensis and A. caudatus

names.name: Zarzaparrilla colorada
names.ambiguity_note: Zarzaparrilla colorada = Muehlenbeckia sagittifolia in Argentina; zarzaparrilla alone in Chile = Ribes cucullatum; neither maps to the classical sarsaparilla (Smilax spp.). Three-way ambiguity

names.name: Salvia morada
names.ambiguity_note: Salvia morada in Argentina = Lippia alba (Verbenaceae), not related to true salvia (Salvia officinalis, Lamiaceae). This is a classic false-friend: the name salvia applied to a completely different family based on aromatic similarity

names.name: Sangre de drago
names.ambiguity_note: Argentine sangre de drago = Croton urucurana; Peruvian/Ecuadorian sangre de grado = Croton lechleri. Related Croton species but distinct; both produce red latex used similarly. Confirmed two-species ambiguity noted in Phase 1

names.name: Sen de campo
names.ambiguity_note: Sen de campo in Argentina = Senna corymbosa (native); sen in Chile = Senna alexandrina (introduced); both are Senna species used as laxatives but distinct

contraindications: Do not ingest — highly toxic alkaloid content (anabasine)
External use with caution only
Avoid during pregnancy
Seeds are highly toxic — contain curcin (toxalbumin)
Do not ingest any part without medical supervision
Avoid during pregnancy
Potentially fatal at high doses
Highly toxic latex — external use only
Do not ingest
Avoid contact with mucous membranes
All parts toxic, especially unripe berries
Do not ingest
Risk of hepatotoxicity at high doses
Avoid during pregnancy
All parts toxic especially roots and berries
Do not ingest roots
Traditional use restricted to bark and very dilute preparations
Avoid during pregnancy
Contains aristolochic acids — nephrotoxic and potentially carcinogenic
Avoid during pregnancy — strong abortifacient activity
Avoid in renal disease
Long-term use contraindicated — may cause aristolochic acid nephropathy
Contains huperzine A — avoid combining with acetylcholinesterase inhibitor drugs
Avoid during pregnancy
Use with medical supervision only
Seeds and latex highly toxic — contain sanguinarine
Seed oil causes epidemic dropsy (Sanguinarine toxicity)
Do not ingest seeds or latex
External use with caution
Hallucinogenic alkaloids (bufotenin, DMT) — legal status varies by country
Avoid during pregnancy
Do not combine with MAOIs
Traditional ritual use only under qualified guidance
Avoid during pregnancy and lactation
High doses may cause nausea and vomiting
Avoid during pregnancy
Avoid in known ulcerative conditions without medical supervision
Avoid leaves in children under 6 at high doses
High leaf tannin content — may cause constipation with excessive use
Avoid excessive consumption — contains caffeine and methylxanthines
Avoid during pregnancy at high doses
Avoid in cardiovascular disease with arrhythmias
Risk of esophageal/oral cancer with very hot mate consumption (thermal injury, not phytochemical)
Contains lyfoline and related alkaloids with CNS-active properties
Avoid during pregnancy
Do not exceed traditional doses
Avoid in cardiac or renal insufficiency
Contains thiaminase — may cause vitamin B1 deficiency with prolonged use
Avoid during pregnancy
Avoid during pregnancy — uterine stimulant activity reported
Avoid in anxiety disorders at high doses
Diabetic patients should monitor blood glucose — may require dose adjustment of antidiabetic drugs
drug_interactions: May potentiate hypoglycemic drugs and insulin

contraindications: Avoid during pregnancy
Avoid in active hemorrhage without medical supervision
Avoid during pregnancy — uterine stimulant activity
Do not confuse with other plants called contrayerba
Do not confuse with Uncaria tomentosa (uña de gato vine) — entirely different pharmacological profile
Avoid during pregnancy
